note_html:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient is anxious about her recent blood work showing hepatitis C, which she was not aware she had despite not having any symptoms. She reports a history of IV drug use in the past, but has been clean for over 15 years. She also reports a history of alcohol use, currently consuming about a beer a day, and smoking one to two cigarettes a day. She denies any other medical conditions, except for a tendency to feel tired at the end of the day after working. Her family history is significant for hypertension, diabetes, and depression. She reports being a workaholic, but is working on addressing this.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of IV drug use, which she has been clean from for over 15 years. She also has a history of alcohol use, currently consuming about a beer a day, and smoking one to two cigarettes a day. She denies any other medical conditions.

Review of systems: The patient reports feeling tired at the end of the day after working, but otherwise denies any other symptoms.

Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The patient appears in no distress and no jaundice on skin exam. The heart exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm with a 2/6 systolic murmur, which has not changed from last year. The lungs are clear with no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. The abdomen is soft with no hepatosplenomegaly. The musculoskeletal exam is unremarkable.

Test Results: The patient's HCV antibody test was positive, and her liver panel showed an elevated AST at 39, but her ALT, albumin, and total bilirubin were within normal limits.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is diagnosed with hepatitis C, a viral infection that affects the liver, which she most likely has had for years.

Problem list: Hepatitis C; Suspected liver disease; Suspected fibrosis of the liver; Suspected hypertension; Suspected diabetes; Suspected depression.

Plan:
I will order additional blood work to confirm the diagnosis of hepatitis C, including checking the HCV RNA and HCV genotype. I will also order a special ultrasound to determine the severity of her liver disease. I will have the nurse schedule follow-up appointments for the patient's wife and children to undergo screening for hepatitis C.

Follow-up: I will see the patient in about three weeks to review the results of the additional blood work and ultrasound, and to determine the next steps in treatment.
